summary: Gruber writes on the eve of WWDC 2013 that Apple has successfully doubled down on secrecy, leaving even well-connected observers completely in the dark about iOS 7. He argues that whatever Jony Ive's team has done to redesign iOS, it should be evaluated not on surface aesthetics but on how it works, invoking Steve Jobs's famous definition of design. The core thesis is that iOS's original skeuomorphic, button-heavy design existed to teach people how to use touchscreens — and now that touchscreen computing is universally understood, those training wheels are no longer needed. He predicts Apple will remove them at WWDC, marking a new phase for iOS design.
key_points: Tim Cook's promise to 'double down on secrecy' was genuine — no one outside Apple knows what iOS 7 looks like before WWDC 2013
Design should be evaluated on how it works, not merely what it looks like, per Steve Jobs's classic definition
iOS's skeuomorphic, overtly tactile design was a deliberate response to touchscreens being unfamiliar — it served as training wheels
Touchscreen computing is now universally understood, so the original design rationale no longer applies
The redesign is driven by solving new problems, not by fashion or change for its own sake
money_quotes: I haven't been this in the dark about what Apple was set to announce in a keynote since the original iPhone introduction in January 2007.
Whatever has changed in iOS 7 has changed for reasons other than fashion, or difference for the sake of difference.
It's not just what it looks like and feels like. Design is how it works.
The design of the iPhone software was entirely informed by the fact that this was a new experience.
Look around you. Any street corner. Any office. Any shopping mall. Any restaurant. You will see people tapping on touchscreens. We all get it now.
iOS-style computing is no longer novel; it is now the standard interaction model for personal computing.
The primary problem Apple faced with the iPhone in 2007 was building familiarity with a new way of using computers. That problem has now been solved. It is time to solve new problems.
The training wheels can now come off. That's what I think Apple's going to do tomorrow.
key_insight: iOS's skeuomorphic design was never about aesthetics but about teaching people to use touchscreens, and with that mission accomplished, Apple is free to strip away the training wheels and redesign for a world that now understands touch computing.
